elements.sent-2411-001: It is known that generic eigenstates of free-fermionic spin chains can be prepared with circuits whose depth grows only polynomially with the length of the chain and the number of particles.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that the polynomial growth is also achievable for selected interacting models where the interaction between the particles is sufficiently simple.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our working example is the folded XXZ model, an integrable spin chain that exhibits Hilbert space fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-004: We present the explicit quantum circuits that prepare arbitrary eigenstates of this model on an open chain efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-005: We perform error-mitigated noisy simulations with circuits of up to 13 qubits and different connectivities between qubits, achieving a relative error below 5\%.
elements.sent-2411-006: As a byproduct, we extend a recent reformulation of the Bethe ansatz as a quantum circuit from closed to open boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Eigenstate preparation is an important challenge in the application of quantum computation to the study of many-body physics.
elements.sent-2411-008: The implementation of eigenstates on a quantum computer would give direct access to their non-local properties, which are notably hard to obtain even resorting to tensor networks techniques, or to exact results for integrable systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: Moreover, eigenstates can be useful for benchmarking as well as the initialization of quantum algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-010: Quantum circuits for eigenstate preparation must be efficient to be implementable, meaning that the number of one- and two-qubit gates must grow polynomially with the number of qubits and/or excitations in the model.
elements.sent-2411-011: There are efficient circuits that prepare free-fermion eigenstates exactly~\cite{Verstraete09,Jiang17,Kivlichan18 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The existence of such algorithms relates to the fact free fermions are classically simulatable~\cite{Valiant02,Terhal02,Jozsa08 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: What is the class of models that admit efficient eigenstate preparation with unitary circuits remains an open question.
elements.sent-2411-014: It is true that ground states and certain excited states can have a relatively simple structure that enables efficient preparation.
elements.sent-2411-015: Conversely, our goal here is to develop algorithms capable of preparing arbitrary excited states in selected models.
elements.sent-2411-016: It is natural to consider integrable spin chains with regard to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-017: Integrable systems are reputed to have simpler eigenstates than generic models, as integrability guarantees the models have completely elastic and factorized scattering processes~\cite{sutherland-book .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The exact wavefunctions, which are computed by the coordinate Bethe Ansatz (CBA), are often known.
elements.sent-2411-019: The associated eigenstates can be usually constructed efficiently by a sequence of non-unitary linear operations, in a procedure known as algebraic Bethe Ansatz (ABA)~\cite{Korepin1993kvr,faddeev1996algebraic .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The search of a unitary counterpart of these protocols has attracted considerable attention in the recent years.
elements.sent-2411-021: The paradigmatic spin chain solvable by the Bethe Ansatz is the XXZ model.
elements.sent-2411-022: Quantum circuits to prepare the eigenstates of the XXZ model with closed~\cite{VanDyke21,Sopena22,Ruiz23, Raveh24 and open boundary conditions~\cite{VanDyke22,Raveh24 have been designed.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: They fall into two classes: probabilistic~\cite{VanDyke21,VanDyke22 and deterministic algorithms~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23,Raveh24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The number of gates of the probabilistic algorithms in~\cite{VanDyke21,VanDyke22 is polynomial in the number of qubits $N$ and excitations (``magnons'') $M$, but their success probability is exponentially small~\cite{Li22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The number of gates of the deterministic algorithm of~\cite{Raveh24 is exponential in the number of magnons.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, the deterministic circuits of~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 involve multi-qubit unitaries, whose decomposition in terms of elementary gates likely suffers from the same problem.}
elements.sent-2411-027: There are thus no known efficient quantum algorithm preparing the XXZ eigenstates.
elements.sent-2411-028: This raises the question of whether a quantum computer can access general eigenstates beyond those of free fermions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our main result is proving that there are interacting many-body systems for which efficient algorithms prepare all their eigenstates.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our strategy is to consider interacting models with relatively simple dynamical features.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, we focus on the strong-coupling limit of the XXZ spin chain, where the dynamics drastically simplifies.
elements.sent-2411-032: There are essentially two ways to define an infinite-coupling limit of this spin chain, both of which can be understood as Schrieffer-Wolff transformation of certain kind.
elements.sent-2411-033: First, following~\cite{xxz-triple-point , the model can be scaled to its triple point, which brings about the ``constrained XX model''.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The interactions of this spin chain consist of contact repulsion among magnons.
elements.sent-2411-035: The related but slightly different strong-coupling limit of~\cite{Zadnik20,Zadnik21,Pozsgay21 leads to the ``folded XXZ model''~\cite{folded-XXZ-stochastic-1,Yang19 , whose excitations are magnons and domain walls.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The Hilbert space of this spin chain splits into exponentially many subsectors with disconnected dynamics, which display the phenomenon called ``Hilbert space fragmentation''~\cite{fragmentation-scars-review-2,hfrag-review .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The folded XXZ model coincides with the constrained XX model in the purely magnonic sector.
elements.sent-2411-038: The simplicity of the folded XXZ model enabled exact computations in a variety of situations~\cite{Pozsgay21,folded-jammed,folded4 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: It is worth mentioning that the folded XXZ model has been realized experimentally in Rydberg atoms~\cite{rydberg-folded-exp1,rydberg-folded-exp2 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: In this paper, we construct a quantum circuit capable of preparing arbitrary eigenstates of the folded XXZ model with open boundaries exactly.
elements.sent-2411-041: The number of gates grows quadratically with the number of sites and linearly with that of excitations, being thus efficient.
elements.sent-2411-042: The structure of the circuit is the following.
elements.sent-2411-043: The initial state fed to the circuit is an $M$-magnon eigenstate of the free XX model.
elements.sent-2411-044: The first step is the application of a unitary $U_0$, which implements contact repulsion in such a way that the initial state is mapped onto an eigenstate of the constrained XX model.
elements.sent-2411-045: The output state then undergoes a second unitary $V_D$ which introduced the domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-046: Both $U_0$ and $V_D$ are built out of $\mathrm{CNOT}$ and $\mathrm{CSWAP}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For the preparation of the XX eigenstates, we generalize the quantum algorithm of~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 , which prepare Bethe states on periodic chains, to open boundary conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We also perform numerical simulations of our circuits for several eigenstates with few excitations and sites.
elements.sent-2411-049: We consider different qubit connectivities and represent noise through a depolarizing channel.

elements.sent-2411-084: Physical processes of the folded XXZ model can be understood as the dynamics of isolated particles and holes together with domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-085: We define these concepts with the help of the computational basis first.
elements.sent-2411-086: We analyze transitions between states afterward.
elements.sent-2411-087: We say that a transition is a link between two qubits such that two neighboring qubits carry different bits.
elements.sent-2411-088: Examples of states with transitions are $\ket{01}$ and $\ket{10}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: A particle is a single $\ket{1}$ embedded into a block $\ket{00\ldots0}$, and a hole is a single $\ket{0}$ embedded into a block $\ket{11\ldots 1}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: For instance, $\ket{00100}$ has a particle at the third position and $\ket{11011}$ a hole at the third position.
elements.sent-2411-091: We use the common term ``magnon'' for both particles and holes.
elements.sent-2411-092: Magnons can be seen as a bound state of two transitions at neighboring positions.
elements.sent-2411-093: The state $\ket{0010011011}$ thus has two magnons: a particle on the third position and a hole on the eight position.
elements.sent-2411-094: Our definition implies that magnons cannot occupy neighboring positions, but they can occupy next-to-nearest neighboring positions.
elements.sent-2411-095: The position of magnons can be ambiguous in certain states.
elements.sent-2411-096: For example, we can say $\ket{1010}$ has two particles at the first and third position or two holes at the second and fourth positions.
elements.sent-2411-097: We resolve the ambiguity by choosing magnons to be at leftmost available configuration, hence $\ket{1010}$ has two particles rather than two holes.
elements.sent-2411-098: A domain is a block of the spin chain with more than one qubit at the same state of the computational basis.
elements.sent-2411-099: The state $\ket{0011}$ then has two domains of length two.
elements.sent-2411-100: A domain wall is a transition between two domains.
elements.sent-2411-101: We do not count transitions associated to magnons as domain walls because domains have more than one qubit.
elements.sent-2411-102: We illustrate the identification of domain walls and magnons in a state in Fig.~\ref{fig:dwmagnon .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Much like the location of magnons themselves, the positions of domain walls is ill-defined if there are magnons in their immediate vicinity.
elements.sent-2411-104: For example, the state $\ket{001011}$ has one magnon and one domain wall, and we can say that the particle is at the third position and the domain wall between the fourth and the fifth position, or a hole at the fourth position and a domain wall between the second and the third position.
elements.sent-2411-105: We resolve the ambiguity by choosing magnons to be at the leftmost available position again.
elements.sent-2411-106: The state $\ket{001011}$ thus has a magnon at the third position and domain wall between the fourth and the fifth positions.

elements.sent-2411-135: Hilbert space fragmentation denotes the split of the Hilbert space splits into exponentially many subspaces, called fragments or sectors, such that all the entries of the Hamiltonian between different fragments vanish~\cite{fragmentation-scars-review-2,hfrag-review .}
elements.sent-2411-136: The definition is physically meaningful if the fragments can be constructed via local constraints or local rules before actually finding all the exact eigenstates.
elements.sent-2411-137: In the folded XXZ model, the fragmentation arises from the kinematic constraint in the hopping processes.
elements.sent-2411-138: Different fragments can be labeled by the numbers of magnons and the number and relative positions of the domain walls.

elements.sent-2411-145: Let us then set open boundary conditions, and let us choose a state $\ket{\psi}$ of the computational basis.
elements.sent-2411-146: The procedure to obtain the labeling is the following.
elements.sent-2411-147: First, we compute the action of the Hamiltonian on the state multiple times $H^n\ket{\psi}$.
elements.sent-2411-148: The result is the Krylov basis of the fragment of $\ket{\psi}$.
elements.sent-2411-149: If we expand $H^n\ket{\psi}$ into the computational basis, the number of terms of the Krylov basis typically increases with $n$.
elements.sent-2411-150: Our goal is to identify a distinguished state of the computational basis appearing at a large enough $n$ that can be used as a label of the fragment.
elements.sent-2411-151: To this aim, we consider all the possible hopping processes which arise from multiple actions of the Hamiltonian, and we focus on a selected direction of the hopping.
elements.sent-2411-152: We move in this way the magnons to their leftmost possible position inside $\ket{\psi}$.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Algebraic Bethe Circuits with Open Boundary Conditions
elements.sent-2411-272: Several quantum circuits to efficiently prepare free-fermion eigenstates are available in the literature~\cite{Verstraete09,Jiang17,Kivlichan18 .}
elements.sent-2411-273: In this paper, we elaborate on the recent proposal of~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 .}
elements.sent-2411-274: These works succeeded to recast the Bethe Ansatz in terms of quantum circuits, named ``Algebraic Bethe Circuits (ABC)''.
elements.sent-2411-275: The growth of the computational resources required by ABC is linear in the number of qubits, but a priori exponential in the number of magnons.
elements.sent-2411-276: However, for the free XX model, the resources scale linearly both in the number of qubits and magnons, matching the efficiency of~\cite{Jiang17,Kivlichan18 .}
elements.sent-2411-277: The results of~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 focused on preparing Bethe states on periodic chains.}
elements.sent-2411-278: We show below that the construction easily generalizes to open boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-279: It is well-known that the ABA~\cite{Korepin1993kvr,faddeev1996algebraic , which renders the underlying integrability explicit, can be reinterpreted in the language of matrix-product states (MPS)~\cite{Alcaraz03,Alcaraz03ii,Alcaraz06,Katsura10,Murg_2012 , the simplest example of one-dimensional tensor network \cite{Cirac20 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: It was shown in~\cite{Ruiz23 that the CBA also admits a natural MPS formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-281: According to~\cite{Ruiz23 , the plane-wave superposition of $M$ magnons on a closed chain of $N$ sites can be rewritten as \begin{equation}|\Psi (p_1,\ldots,p_M)\rangle= \e^{\im {\cal P}_M} \sum_{i_j=0,1} \langle 0|^{\otimes M} \Lambda^{i_N} \ldots \;\Lambda^{i_1}| 1 \rangle^{\otimes M} | i_1 \dots i_{N} \rangle \ , \label{eigenCBA}\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ is the three-leg tensor characteristic of MPS formulations.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Once the physical index $i$ is fixed, the tensor $\Lambda$ acts as a matrix on an auxiliary space, which for an $M$-magnon state has dimension $2^M$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Remarkably, the CBA tensor $\Lambda$ was obtained from a simple set of diagrammatic rules whose input are the magnon momenta and their associated scattering amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-284: The normalization of the CBA wavefunctions in~\cite{Ruiz23 slightly differs from the one here.}
elements.sent-2411-285: The exponential of the total magnon momentum ${\cal P _M$ in the right-hand side of \eqref{eigenCBA is the proportionality factor between both normalizations.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: The CBA tensor can be seen as a $M\!+\!1$-qubit transformation $\bar \Lambda$ by identifying \begin{equation}\Lambda_{{\vec \alpha}{\vec \beta}}^{i_k}\equiv {\bar \Lambda}_{{\vec \alpha}0}^{i_k {\vec \beta}} = \langle i_k \,{\vec \beta}| {\bar \Lambda} | {\vec \alpha} \,0 \rangle \ , \quad\end{equation} with ${\vec \alpha =[\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_M]$ and ${\vec \beta =[\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_M]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: We can then interpret~\eqref{eigenCBA as a circuit with the staircase structure shown in Fig.~\ref{figclosedCBA , which acts on $N+M$ qubits initialized in the state $|1\rangle^{\otimes M |0\rangle^{\otimes N-M}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Nonetheless, there are two obvious problems with this interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-289: First, the $M$ rightmost output qubits should be postselected at $|0\rangle$ to obtain $|\Psi_M\rangle$, which implies an exponential shot cost in the number of magnons.
elements.sent-2411-291: These issues can be addressed by using the large gauge freedom of the MPS \cite{Cirac20 .}
elements.sent-2411-292: The transformation \begin{equation}\Lambda^{i_k} \mapsto X_{k+1} \,\Lambda^{i_k} X^{-1}_{k} \label{MPSgauge}\end{equation} where $X_k$ are arbitrary invertible matrices acting on the auxiliary space, together with \begin{equation}|1\rangle^{\otimes M} \mapsto X_1 |1\rangle^{\otimes M} \ , \langle 0|^{\otimes M} \mapsto \langle 0|^{\otimes M} X_{N+1}^{-1} \ , \label{leftover}\end{equation} leaves~\eqref{eigenCBA invariant.}

elements.sent-2411-474: In this paper, we constructed a quantum algorithm capable of preparing all the eigenstates of the folded XXZ model with open boundaries, containing both magnons and domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-475: To the best of our knowledge, it is the first efficient quantum algorithm that prepares every eigenstate of an interacting quantum spin chain.
elements.sent-2411-476: Along the way, we extended the reformulation of the Bethe ansatz in terms of quantum circuits, termed ABC in~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 , from closed to open chains.}
elements.sent-2411-477: The construction of the circuit relies on the description of eigenstates with magnons and domain walls of Section~\ref{sec:eigenstates , which uses as seed an eigenstate of the XX model on a reduced number of sites.}
elements.sent-2411-478: We proposed to prepare the XX eigenstate using the open boundary generalization of the ABC.
elements.sent-2411-479: The magnon contact repulsion is then implemented through the simple coordinate shift \eqref{shift .}
elements.sent-2411-480: Subsequently, the domain walls are displaced from their reference position following the rules \eqref{reloc and \eqref{displace , which automatically reveal whether a magnon is to be represented as a particle or a hole.}}
elements.sent-2411-481: Even though this picture is equivalent to the effective coordinates of~\cite{Pozsgay21,sajat-hardrod , it offers a simpler description of the folded XXZ eigenstates which could help to construct eigenstates of other models with similar dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-482: Our circuits require ancillas, especially relevant to the eigenstates that have domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-483: To estimate the performance of circuits on NISQ computers, we ran numerical simulations including a simple depolarizing noise model.
elements.sent-2411-484: Since qubit connectivity has a strong impact on the fidelity of gates between distant qubits, we have considered two different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-485: First, an all-to-all connectivity, typical of trapped-ion and Rydberg-atom prototypes.
elements.sent-2411-486: Second, a square-lattice connectivity, as implemented in Google's superconducting chips.
elements.sent-2411-487: Our simulations indicate that simple eigenstates, containing few magnons and domain walls, could be implemented with acceptable fidelity on quantum computers with two-qubit gate fidelity below $10^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-488: Therefore, our algorithm is a good candidate for benchmarking applications.
elements.sent-2411-489: Hilbert space fragmentation~\cite{fragmentation-scars-review-2,hfrag-review has attracted much attention in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-490: The reason is that it describes a new type of ergodicity breakdown, hence of violation of the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-491: Our results open the way to study of this phenomenon on a digital quantum computer, adding to previous proposals on a Rydberg-atom simulator~\cite{rydberg-folded-exp1,rydberg-folded-exp2 .}
elements.sent-2411-492: For example, it would be interesting to explore the evolution and mixing of folded XXZ fragments under perturbations of varying strength.
elements.sent-2411-493: One could use the fact that our algorithm allows preparing not only eigenstates, but also superpositions of eigenstates from different fragments.
elements.sent-2411-494: These superpositions can be easily implemented by initializing the physical register $R_{\mathrm{phys }$ of the circuit in Subsection~\ref{addingDW in a superposition of reference-states $|\Psi_{0,D}\rangle$.}}
elements.sent-2411-495: The resulting output could then be fed to a quantum circuit realizing the Trotterized evolution under the desired perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-496: Finally, it is clearly very interesting to search for other spin chain models whose spectrum can be efficiently prepared on a quantum computer.
elements.sent-2411-497: The circuits presented in this paper straightforwardly generalize to the class of constrained XX models of~\cite{Alcaraz99,constrained3,Alcaraz07 , in which contact repulsion among magnons extends to $\ell$ sites.}
elements.sent-2411-498: Another class of interesting candidates are the recently found models of hidden free fermions~\cite{Fendley19,unified-graph-th,sajat-FP-model .}
elements.sent-2411-499: Although these models seemingly display interaction, their Hamiltonians have a free-fermion spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-500: However, the associated fermionic operators do not follow from a Jordan-Wigner transformation.

elements.section-2411-008: Algebraic Bethe Circuits for Free Fermions
elements.sent-2411-555: The unitaries $P_k$ of ABC for free fermions with open boundaries of Subsection~\ref{ssOFFABC are layers of two-qubit unitaries, matchgates in particular~\cite{Ruiz23,code_abc , and a single-qubit rotation for the small unitaries.}}
elements.sent-2411-556: Fig.~\ref{figMGL --\ref{figMGS shows the factorization of unitaries into matchgates.}}
elements.sent-2411-557: In this appendix, we provide closed formulae one- and two-qubit gates that did not appear in~\cite{Ruiz23 .}
elements.sent-2411-558: We also provide a new proof of their unitarity that does not rely on the connection with ABC.
elements.sent-2411-559: Matchgates are based on the Bethe states of one magnon with one quasi-momentum~$q_a$ in $q_1,\ldots,q_{2M}$ over $k$ qubits, where the quasi-momenta are~\eqref{qqmoms .}
elements.sent-2411-560: These Bethe states read \begin{equation}|\Psi_{k,a}\rangle := \overset{k}{\underset{n=1}{\sum}}\, y_{a}^{n-1}\, \sigma_n^{-}\ket{0}^{\otimes k}\end{equation} where $y_a$ are~\eqref{yvars .}
elements.sent-2411-561: If $1\leq k <2M$, there are $k$ linearly independent Bethe states.
elements.sent-2411-562: We choose them to be the Bethe states of quasi-momenta $q_1,\ldots,q_k$.
elements.sent-2411-563: If $2M\leq k <{N}$, there are $M$ linearly independent Bethe states, associated to the quasi-momenta $q_1,\ldots,q_{2M}$.
elements.sent-2411-564: The Gram matrix of the Bethe states over $k$ qubits thus chosen is positive definite and Hermitian.. Let \begin{equation}L_k:=\min(k,2M) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-565: The Gram matrix of the Bethe states reads \begin{equation}\label{Gram} C_k = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} C_{12} \ldots C_{1L_k} C_{21} C_{22} \ldots C_{2L_k} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots C_{L_k1} C_{L_k2} \ldots C_{L_kL_k} \end{bmatrix} \ ,\end{equation} where the entries are \begin{equation}\label{Gramentries} C_{k,ab} := \langle \Psi_{k,a} | \Psi_{k,b}\rangle \ .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-077: \begin{equation}\label{Gram} C_k =  C_{11} C_{12} \ldots C_{1L_k} C_{21} C_{22} \ldots C_{2L_k} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots C_{L_k1} C_{L_k2} \ldots C_{L_kL_k}  \ ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-566: The matchgates of the quantum circuit are two-qubit unitaries based on the Gram matrix: \begin{equation}\label{Fkj} F_{k,a}= \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 0 0 {u}^*_{k,a} v_{k,a} 0 0 -{v}^*_{k,a} u_{k,a} 0 0 0 0 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad 1\leq a<L_k \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-567: The entries are \begin{equation}\label{uvmatchgate} \begin{split} u_{k,a}=\frac{(-1)^{a-1}}{y_1^* \ldots y_{a-1}^*} \frac{{\det}_a C_{k+1, a\rightarrow e}}{\sqrt{{\det}_{a-1}C_k {\det}_a C_{k+1}}} \ , \quad v_{k,a}=y_a \sqrt{\frac{{\det}_a C_k{\det}_{a-1}C_{k+1}}{{\det}_{a-1}C_k {\det}_a C_{k+1}}} \ , \end{split}\end{equation} where $\det_a C_k$ denotes the upper-left minor of order $a$ of $C_k$ and ${C _{k,a\rightarrow e}$ denotes the matrix that results from the replacement of the $a$-th row of ${C _k$ by \begin{equation}e=\begin{bmatrix} 1 1 \dots 1 \end{bmatrix} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\label{uvmatchgate}  u_{k,a}=\frac{(-1)^{a-1}}{y_1^* \ldots y_{a-1}^*} \frac{{\det}_a C_{k+1, a\rightarrow e}}{\sqrt{{\det}_{a-1}C_k {\det}_a C_{k+1}}} \ , \quad v_{k,a}=y_a \sqrt{\frac{{\det}_a C_k{\det}_{a-1}C_{k+1}}{{\det}_{a-1}C_k {\det}_a C_{k+1}}} \ , \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-568: The quantum circuit also comprises the single-qubit rotation \begin{equation}\label{1qU} U_k= \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 \exp(\im\phi_k) \end{bmatrix} \ ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{phase} \exp(\im\phi_k)=\prod_{a=1}^{k}\sqrt{\frac{y_{k+1}-y_a}{{y}^*_{k+1}-{y}^*_a}} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-570: It remains to demonstrate the matchgates~\eqref{Fkj are unitary as well.}
elements.sent-2411-571: Unitarity is equivalent to \begin{equation}\label{uncond} |{\det}_a\!\Cc_{k+1,a\to e}\!|^2\!-|y_1\dots y_{a-1}|^2{\det}_{a-1}\Cc_{k} {\det}_a\Cc_{k+1}+|y_1\dots y_a|^2{\det}_a\Cc_{k}{\det}_{a-1}\Cc_{k+1} =0 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-572: The first step we take is the use of the recursion relation \begin{equation}\label{recursion} C_{k+1,ab}={y}^*_{a}y_{b}C_{k,ab}+1 \ , \quad C_{0,ab}=1 \ ,\end{equation} to write \begin{equation}|y_1\dots y_a|^2{\det}_a\Cc_k={\det}_a(\Cc_{k+1}-E) \ ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}E= \begin{bmatrix} 1 1 \ldots 1 1 1 \ldots 1 \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots 1 1 \ldots 1 \end{bmatrix} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-573: Since the subscript $k+1$ is fixed until the end of the proof, we define \begin{equation}\Cc:= \Cc_{k+1} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-574: We use the identity of block-diagonal matrices \begin{equation}\label{identity} \det \begin{bmatrix} P Q R S \end{bmatrix} = \det S{\det} (P-RS^{-1}Q) \quad \mathrm{if} \quad \!\!\! \det S \neq 0 \ ,\end{equation} to write \begin{equation}\label{algids} \begin{split} \frac{{\det}_a\Cc_{a\to e}}{\det_{a-1}\Cc}=1-e^t C_{a-1}^{-1} c_a \ , \frac{{\det}_a\Cc}{\det_{a-1}\Cc}=C_{aa}-c_a^\dagger C_{a-1}^{-1}c_a \ , \frac{{\det}_a(\Cc-E)}{\det_{a-1}(\Cc-E)}=C_{aa}-1-(c_a-e)^\dagger (C_{a-1}-E)^{-1}(c_a-e) \ , \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}c_a = \begin{bmatrix} C_{a1} C_{a2} \ldots C_{aa-1} \end{bmatrix} \ , \quad C_a-1 = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} C_{12} \ldots C_{1a-1} C_{21} C_{22} \ldots C_{2a-1} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots C_{a-11} C_{a-12} \ldots C_{a-1a-1} \end{bmatrix} \ .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-641: The quantum circuit prepares normalized Bethe states of the XX model with $M$ magnons and free open boundaries over $N_0$ sites, namely \begin{equation}|\widehat{\Phi}_M\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\langle\Phi_M |\Phi_M\rangle}} \sum_{\epsilon_a=\pm1} \left[\, \prod_{b=1}^M \epsilon_b \right]|\Phi(\epsilon_1 p_1,\ldots, \epsilon_M p_M ) \rangle \ , \label{superp2}\end{equation} where~$|\Phi(p_1,\ldots,p_M)\rangle$ denote the Bethe states of the periodic XX model, whose wavefunctions are given in~\eqref{Slater .}

elements.section-2411-011: Applications of the Quantum Algorithm to Other Models
elements.sent-2411-674: In this section, we show that our quantum algorithm is not limited to the folded XXZ model, but can also be applied to other problems.
elements.sent-2411-675: Consider the expression \eqref{wavefunct for the preparation of the eigenstates of the folded XXZ model.}
elements.sent-2411-676: Our key result was the construction of an efficient quantum circuit for the operator $U_D$.
elements.sent-2411-677: This operator performs a ``hard-rod deformation'' and it also adds domain walls to the state.
elements.sent-2411-678: The hard-rod deformation can be understood as adding extra length to the particles by replacing every occurrence of a local configuration $\ket{1}$ in the computational basis by $\ket{01}$.
elements.sent-2411-679: To obtain the eigenstates of the folded XXZ model, we applied $U_D$ to an eigenstate of the XX model.
elements.sent-2411-680: However, we can also consider the action of $U_D$ on other states, thereby revealing other applications of our quantum algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-681: For example, we can act with $U_D$ on eigenstates of the XXZ model.
elements.sent-2411-682: This way we can prepare eigenstates of the so-called ``hard-rod deformed XXZ models'' (see \cite{sajat-hardrod ).}
elements.sent-2411-683: Currently, there are no efficient formulas for eigenstate preparation in the XXZ chain; nevertheless, eigenstates can be prepared in small volumes or with a small number of magnons, and our algorithm can immediately give the eigenstates of the hard-rod deformed XXZ model as well.
elements.sent-2411-684: Another application, somewhat independent of the previous computations, is the preparation of a modified version of Dicke states.
elements.sent-2411-685: The Dicke state $\ket{\mathcal{D _M}$ is defined as the equal weight superposition of the basis states that have $M$ excitations over a ferromagnetic vacuum.}
elements.sent-2411-686: By applying our hard-rod deformation algorithm, specialized to zero domain walls, to the states $\ket{\mathcal{D _M}$, we obtain ``constrained Dicke states''.}
elements.sent-2411-687: These states are an equal weight superposition of all basis states that have $M$ excitations such that none of them are on neighbouring sites.
elements.sent-2411-688: Since efficient methods for preparing Dicke states are already known \cite{deterministic-dicke , our algorithm have access to constrained Dicke states as well.}
elements.sent-2411-689: One application of constrained Dicke sates is in the preparation of a selected zero-energy eigenstate of the celebrated PXP model, derived recently in \cite{crosscap-pxp .}
elements.sent-2411-690: This special eigenstate is built on an equal-weight superposition of double tensor products of constrained Dicke states.
elements.sent-2411-691: Our algorithms can be generalized to prepare the desired final state.
elements.sent-2411-692: This could serve as a compelling first example of an exactly prepared eigenstate for the PXP model.
elements.sent-2411-693: We now summarize our results and place them in a broader context, highlighting potential applications to benchmarking, quantum advantage, and the study of Hilbert space fragmentation, complementing the discussion of the application of our methods to other models and special states in Section~6.
elements.sent-2411-694: Along the way, we extended the reformulation of the Bethe Ansatz in terms of quantum circuits, termed ABC in~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 , from closed to open chains.}
elements.sent-2411-695: Since qubit connectivity has a strong impact on the error of gates between distant qubits, we have considered two different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-696: Our simulations indicate that simple eigenstates, containing few magnons and domain walls, could be implemented with acceptable fidelity on quantum computers with two-qubit gate {error below $10^{-3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-697: Our results contribute to the possibility of studying this phenomenon on a digital quantum computer.
elements.sent-2411-698: For example, it would be interesting to explore the evolution and mixing of the fragments under perturbations {that undo the fragmentation .}
elements.sent-2411-699: As a further potential application, our circuits can be used both for benchmarking and for demonstrating quantum advantage in selected problems.
elements.sent-2411-700: The idea is to measure certain local and non-local correlation functions and compare them to results from classical computations.
elements.sent-2411-701: When compact exact results are available, experimental outcomes could serve as reliable benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-702: Perhaps more interestingly, our method enables access to quantities for which no closed-form classical results are known, but which could now be explored experimentally on quantum devices.
elements.sent-2411-703: The folded XXZ model is a recently discovered integrable model, for which there are relatively few exact results available for local or non-local correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-704: One notable exception is the so-called emptiness formation probability in states without domain walls, which is analytically known and directly measurable; see, for example, Section~XI of~\cite{Pozsgay21 .}
elements.sent-2411-705: This quantity could serve as a practical benchmark on quantum hardware.
elements.sent-2411-706: In contrast, many other correlation functions, both local and non-local, do not admit compact analytic expressions, even though the wavefunctions are known via the Bethe Ansatz.
elements.sent-2411-707: This is in fact a very common phenomenon: it is notoriously difficult to exactly compute non-local correlation functions also in the Heisenberg XXZ model, especially at intermediate distances.
elements.sent-2411-708: Although classical computation is in principle possible using the exact Bethe wave functions, the evaluation of correlation functions requires summing over an exponentially large number of terms.
elements.sent-2411-709: Therefore, in the context of the folded XXZ model, the combination of efficient eigenstate preparation and quantum measurement of a non-local observable would constitute a concrete demonstration of quantum supremacy.
elements.sent-2411-710: Most of the eigenstates that we treated serve as concrete examples of highly entangled, highly non-Clifford, and pure and real quantum states that can be efficiently cross-device verified~\cite{Elben20, Carrasco21, Anshu22 .}
elements.sent-2411-711: While cross-device verification is typically intended for comparing arbitrary quantum states across different quantum computers, these particular states provide a compelling test case due to their complex entanglement structure and the difficulty of their classical simulation.
elements.sent-2411-712: Additionally, these states are relevant to quantum cryptography, as they allow two remote parties to verify that they share the same quantum state without revealing enough information to identify the state itself, aligning with secure quantum verification protocols where minimal information exchange is required~\cite{Denzler25 .}
elements.sent-2411-713: Thus, while the primary motivation for preparing these states lies in their role in quantum many-body physics, their structured nature makes them valuable tools for benchmarking quantum devices and advancing cryptographic verification techniques.
elements.sent-2411-714: The authors are thankful to Jos{\'e Carrasco, Germ{\'a n Sierra, Egor Tiunov, Santiago Varona, and Hua-Cheng Zhang for stimulating discussions.}}
elements.section-2411-013: Algebraic Bethe Circuits with Open Boundary Conditions
elements.sent-2411-715: In this appendix, we present ABC with open boundary conditions of Subsection~\ref{ssOFFABC .}
elements.sent-2411-716: References~\cite{Sopena22,Ruiz23 introduced ABC as quantum circuits constructed with multi-qubit unitaries that prepare Bethe states of spin-$1/2$ chains with periodic boundary conditions and $U(1)$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-717: It is well-known that the ABA~\cite{Korepin1993kvr,faddeev1996algebraic can be formulated in terms of MPS~\cite{Alcaraz03,Alcaraz03ii,Alcaraz06,Katsura10,Murg_2012 , the simplest class of one-dimensional tensor networks~\cite{Cirac20 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-718: The key behind the ABC in~\cite{Ruiz23 is the representation of Bethe states in the CBA as translationally invariant MPS.}
elements.sent-2411-719: The MPS appeared in~\cite{Alcaraz03,Alcaraz03ii,Alcaraz06 (up to the normalization), but the connection with the CBA remained unnoticed.}
elements.sent-2411-720: As shown in~\cite{Ruiz23 , the Bethe state of $M$ magnons in a closed spin-$1/2$ chain with $N$ qubits is \begin{equation}|\Psi (p_1,\ldots,p_M)\rangle= \e^{\im {\cal P}_M} \sum_{i_j=0,1} \langle 0|^{\otimes M} \Lambda^{i_N} \ldots \;\Lambda^{i_1}| 1 \rangle^{\otimes M} | i_1 \dots i_{N} \rangle \ , \label{eigenCBA}\end{equation} where $p_1,\ldots,p_M$ are the momenta of the magnons, and $\Lambda$ is the tensor defining the MPS.}
elements.sent-2411-721: The tensor $\Lambda$ acts on $M$ ancillas, producing one physical qubit in the state $|i\rangle$ alongside $M$ ancillas.
elements.sent-2411-722: It has one physical index $i$ and two auxiliary indices.
elements.sent-2411-723: For fixed $i$, the tensor is $\Lambda^i$, a $2^M \times 2^M$ matrix in the auxiliary space.
elements.sent-2411-724: Moreover,~\cite{Ruiz23 highlighted that, if the MPS is initialized in $|n_1\ldots n_r\rangle$ instead of $|1\rangle^{\otimes M}$, (\ref{eigenCBA ) prepares Bethe states with $1 \leq r < M$ magnons and momenta $p_{n_1 ,\ldots,p_{n_r}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-725: Let us also introduce the momenta variables \begin{equation}\label{yvars} y_a=\exp(\im q_a) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-726: The tensor $\Lambda$ of ABC with open boundary conditions is borrowed from~\cite{Ruiz23 .}
elements.sent-2411-727: The tensor $\Lambda$ acts on $2M$ ancillas and produces one physical qubit in the state $|i\rangle$ and $2M$ ancillas, and reads~\footnote{Formula~\eqref{tensorMPS matches (55) and (57) of~\cite{Ruiz23 if the number of qubits is $2M$, $x_a$ replace $y_a$, and~$s_{ab}$ replace $B_{ab}$. \begin{equation}\label{tensorMPS} \begin{split} \Lambda^0 = \bigotimes_{a=1}^{2M} \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 y_a \end{bmatrix} \ , \quad \Lambda^1= \sum_{a=1}^{2M} \overset{a-1}{\underset{b=1}{\bigotimes}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 -B(q_b,q_a)y_b \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 1 0 0 \end{bmatrix} \overset{2M}{\underset{c=a+1}{\bigotimes}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 B(q_c,q_a)y_c \end{bmatrix} \ . \end{split}\end{equation} Note $\Lambda$ is not unitary.}}}
elements.sent-2411-728: We can use~\eqref{eigenCBA to write \begin{equation}\begin{split} |\Psi(q_{n_1}\ldots,q_{n_M})\rangle =\e^{\im\mathcal{Q}_a}\sum_{i_j=0,1} \langle 0|^{\otimes 2M} \Lambda^{i_{N}} \ldots \;\Lambda^{i_1}|n_1\ldots n_M\rangle | i_1 \ldots i_{N} \rangle =\underset{1\leq \ell_1<\ldots<\ell_{M}\leq {N}}{\sum} \vphantom{\left[\sum_{\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_M=1}^{2M} \right]} \sum_{\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_{M}=1}^{M} \epsilon_{\beta_1 \ldots \beta_{M}} \left[\underset{1\leq b<a\leq M}{\prod}B(\widetilde{q}_{\beta_a},\widetilde{q}_{\beta_b}) \right] \vphantom{\left[\sum_{\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_M=1}^M \right]} \left[\vphantom{\underset{1\leq a<b\leq M}{\prod}} \!\overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\prod}} \,\,\widetilde{y}_{\beta_a}^{\, \ell_a}\right]\vphantom{\left[\sum_{\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_M=1}^M \right]} \vphantom{\left[\sum_{\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_M=1}^M \right]} | \ell_1 \ldots \ell_M \rangle \ , \end{split} \label{partialapp}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{qmomtot} \e^{\im\mathcal{Q}_a}=\prod_{a=1}^M\e^{\im q_{n_a}} \ ,\end{equation} $1\leq n_1<\ldots < n_M\leq 2M$ and we defined $\widetilde{q _{a :=q_{n_a}$ and $\widetilde{y _a=y_{n_a}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-729: To forbid~\eqref{partialapp from carrying momenta $q_{2a =p_a$ and $q_{2a-1 =-p_a$, we must impose $n_{2a-1}\neq n_{2a}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-730: Bethe states also contain self-scattering amplitudes if the boundaries are open.
elements.sent-2411-731: These amplitudes arise from the interference between the incident and reflected plane waves of a single magnon.
elements.sent-2411-732: For instance, the self-scattering amplitude of the XXZ model with open boundary conditions is \begin{equation}\label{alpha} \alpha(q_a)=1-\Delta y_a^{-1} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-733: The Bethe state of the CBA with open boundary conditions reads \begin{equation}|\Psi_M\rangle= \sum_{\epsilon_a=\pm1} \left[\, \prod_{b=1}^M \epsilon_b \alpha(\epsilon_b p_b) \right] |\Psi(\epsilon_1 p_1,\ldots, \epsilon_M p_M ) \rangle\ . \label{openBethe}\end{equation} Self-scattering requires modifying the tensor network compared to the MPS of Bethe states with periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-734: We modify the MPS through a boundary operator.
elements.sent-2411-735: The boundary operator is a layer of (unnormalized) two-qubit rotations of the sector with one qubit at $\ket{1}$ \begin{equation}O_a= \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 0 0 0 {\alpha}(q_{2a-1})^* -{\alpha}(q_{2a}) 0 0 {\alpha}(q_{2a})^* {\alpha}(q_{2a-1}) 0 0 0 0 1 \end{bmatrix} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-736: The boundary operator introduces self-scattering amplitudes when the MPS is initialized in the N{\'e el state like \begin{equation}|10\rangle^{\otimes M} \mapsto \left[\overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\bigotimes}}\, O_a\right]|10\rangle^{\otimes M}= \overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\bigotimes}}\left[\alpha_{2a-1}|10\rangle - \alpha_{2a}|01\rangle \right] \ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-737: Let us define $\Lambda=\bar{\Lambda}\ket{0}$, where $\bar{\Lambda}$ acts on one physical qubit and $2M$ ancillas and produces one physical qubit in the state $|i\rangle$ along with $2M$ ancillas.
elements.sent-2411-738: Here, $\ket{0}$ represents the input state of the physical qubit.
elements.sent-2411-739: The MPS realization of~\eqref{openBethe forms a staircase of $\bar{\Lambda}$~\cite{Ruiz23 that acts on the N{\'e el state rotated by $B$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-740: Fig.~\ref{figTN depicts the corresponding tensor network.}
elements.sent-2411-741: The tensor network resembles a circuit acting on $N+M$ qubits initialized in $|1\rangle^{\otimes M |0\rangle^{\otimes N-M}$ and preparing Bethe states.}
elements.sent-2411-742: However, the equivalence with a quantum circuit does not hold exactly.
elements.sent-2411-744: Second, the $M$ rightmost output qubits must be postselected at $|0\rangle$ to obtain $|\Psi_M\rangle$, leading to an exponential shot cost in $M$.
elements.sent-2411-745: Both problems can be addressed by exploiting the large gauge freedom of MPS~\cite{Cirac20 .}
elements.sent-2411-746: In particular, we can use this local gauge freedom to transform the tensor network of Fig.~\ref{figTN into a quantum circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-747: The action of the gauge transformation on \eqref{tensorMPS is \begin{equation}\Lambda^{i_k} \mapsto X_{k+1} \,\Lambda^{i_k} X^{-1}_{k} \ , \label{MPSgauge}\end{equation} while the action on the boundary is \begin{equation}\begin{split} \overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\bigotimes}}\left[\alpha_{2a-1}|10\rangle - \alpha_{2a}|01\rangle \right] \mapsto X_1\ \overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\bigotimes}}\left[\alpha_{2a-1}|10\rangle - \alpha_{2a}|01\rangle \right] \ , \quad \langle 0|^{\otimes 2M} \mapsto \langle 0|^{\otimes 2M} X_{N+1}^{-1} \ . \end{split}\end{equation} Since the gauge transformation preserves the $U(1)$ symmetry, the action of $X_{N ^{-1}$ on $\langle 0|^{\otimes M}$ introduces a multiplicative factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-748: The elimination of the ancillas, which can be performed rigorously~\cite{Ruiz24 , leads to the multi-qubit unitaries $P_k$ of ABC.}
elements.sent-2411-749: The action of $X_1$ on the boundary sets our case apart from~\cite{Ruiz23 .}
elements.sent-2411-750: It requires the ABC with open boundary conditions to incorporate a $2M$-qubit unitary $B$ at the boundary, ensuring the proper initialization of the staircase of $P_k$ on the normalized state $|\psi_{\mathrm{in }\rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes N-2M}$.}
elements.sent-2411-751: Specifically,~$B$ must accomplish \begin{equation}|\psi_{\mathrm{in}}\rangle=B|10\rangle^{\otimes M}= \rho X_1 \overset{M}{\underset{a=1}{\bigotimes}}\left(\e^{\im q_{2a-1}}\alpha_{2a-1}|10\rangle - \e^{\im q_{2a}}\alpha_{2a}|01\rangle \right) \ ,\end{equation} where $\rho$ is the normalization factor ensuring $\langle\psi_{\mathrm{in |\psi_{\mathrm{in }\rangle=1$ and we enclosed the contribution of total momentum~(\ref{qmomtot ) in the boundary operator.}}}}
